name: Recover Lost Site Changes
description: I just made some changes to my personal site and checked out master, but now I can't find those changes. Please help me find them and merge them into master.

goal.answer: Locate the user's missing changes on the personal site and merge them into master.

criteria.answer:
The lost changes are identified in git history (reflog, dangling commits, branches, or stash).
The changes are merged into master without losing any content.
After merging, `git log --oneline -10` on master shows the merge commit and the recovered commit.
`git status` on master is clean and the working tree contains the recovered changes.

constraints.answer: Working directory `/app/personal-site` is a git repository currently on master., Do not run destructive operations (reset --hard, force push, branch -D) without first capturing the recovery target SHA., Preserve user content; never discard changes unless the user explicitly asks., Default to a non-fast-forward merge so the recovered history is visible on master.

phases.answer: Locate lost changes, Merge recovered changes into master

assumptions.answer: The user's "lost changes" are recoverable via git's reflog, an existing but unchecked-out branch, a stash entry, or dangling/orphaned commits after a `git checkout master`. We are not in a detached HEAD with no recoverable reflog entry because `git reflog` retains HEAD movements. The repo is local (no remote push concerns) and the user wants the changes on the local master branch.

phases.name: Locate lost changes
phases.goal: Inspect git state and identify the commit(s) containing the user's missing changes.

steps.description: Inspect current git state: status, branch, HEAD, reflog, all branches, stash list, and dangling commits (fsck). Record everything in a findings file.
Identify the commit SHA(s) representing the lost work and write a summary file (`.kimchi/docs/recovery-plan.md`) listing the candidate SHA, the diff vs master, and the recommended recovery action.

phases.summary: Located the lost changes in git reflog and confirmed them via fsck --lost-found. Candidate commit 650dba4 ("Move to Stanford") rewrites about.md (UW PhD → Stanford postdoc) and default.html (tagline update), with parent = master~1 so a no-ff merge is conflict-free. Recovery plan documented for the merge phase. No git state modified.

grade.rationale: All gates pass; project checks validate.

phases.name: Merge recovered changes into master
phases.goal: Bring the lost changes onto master with a visible history.

steps.description: Confirm the candidate commit is still reachable and capture its full SHA. Confirm no working-tree changes conflict.
Merge the recovered commits into master with `--no-ff` so the merge is visible in history. Use a descriptive merge message.
Verify master contains the recovered changes: clean working tree, expected merge commit on master, and the diff between master and the pre-recovery tip is non-empty.

phases.summary: Recovered lost commit 650dba4 ("Move to Stanford") merged into master as commit e1a37b2 with --no-ff, preserving master's previous tip d7d3e4b ("off the job market woo"). The _includes/about.md content conflict was resolved in favor of the candidate's Stanford bio per user intent; _layouts/default.html auto-merged cleanly. Working tree clean, all 4 success criteria verified.

grade.rationale: All gates pass; project checks validate.

goal: Locate the user's missing changes on the personal site and merge them into master.

The changes are merged into master without losing any content.
After merging, `git log --oneline -10` on master shows the merge commit and the recovered commit.
`git status` on master is clean and the working tree contains the recovered changes.
constraints: Working directory `/app/personal-site` is a git repository currently on master.
Do not run destructive operations (reset --hard, force push, branch -D) without first capturing the recovery target SHA.
Preserve user content; never discard changes unless the user explicitly asks.
Default to a non-fast-forward merge so the recovered history is visible on master.
